metadata.summary: 7-step framework to improve activation: define the event correlated with D30+ retention, measure baseline, map funnel, find drop-offs, reduce friction, iterate with A/B tests.

content.title: Activation Framework & Path Optimization
content.summary: A 7-step framework for diagnosing and improving activation rate: define the activation event (behavior correlated with D30+ retention), measure baseline, map the funnel step by step, find the biggest drop-offs, reduce friction, increase motivation, and iterate with A/B tests. Correct activation event definition is the most critical and most commonly wrong step.

why.p: Most SaaS products lose 60-80% of signups before activation. The framework provides a systematic approach rather than guessing which tactic to apply first. The hardest part is defining the right activation event — it must be a behavior that predicts long-term retention, not a milestone that merely signals engagement (e.g. "completed onboarding" is less predictive than "shared a doc within 48h").

list.item: Signups are coming in but week-1 retention is mediocre and the bottleneck is unknown.
Choosing or validating an activation event ("aha moment") that should correlate with D30 retention.
Mapping a funnel, instrumenting missing events, and prioritizing which drop-off to attack first.
Building a weekly activation dashboard and experiment backlog.
Pre-launch: design activation into onboarding from day one; no remediation framework needed.
Pre-PMF: low activation may signal wrong product or wrong audience — fix the product first, not the funnel.
Sales-led B2B: activation happens in human-led implementation; CSM playbooks beat self-serve frameworks.
Single-session utility tools where every visit is an activation.

section.name: activation-framework-7-steps
section.p: Systematic process from defining the activation event through iterative optimization, with funnel mapping example.

rule.statement: Define activation as the specific behavior that correlates most strongly with D30+ retention — not as completing onboarding or creating an account.
rule.rationale: Internal milestones ("completed setup wizard") are easy to optimize but predict nothing. Retention-correlated behaviors ("shared a doc within 48h", "ran 2 reports within 7d") are the real signal.

example.note: Activation = "completed 5-step onboarding wizard" — optimizing this can increase completion 40% with zero retention improvement.

Activation = "added 3 contacts within 24h" — users who do this have 3x D30 retention vs those who do not. Validate by comparing retention curves for users who did vs did not perform the event within N days of signup.

rule.statement: Calculate baseline activation rate and time-to-activation before making any changes; segment by acquisition channel to identify if the problem is channel-specific.
rule.rationale: Without a baseline, you cannot measure improvement. Channel segmentation often reveals that the problem is concentrated in one source (e.g. paid ads have 15% activation vs organic at 45%).

example.code:
Email tool funnel mapping: Step 1: Signup 100% Step 2: Verify email 70% (-30% — highest drop) Step 3: Connect account 50% (-20%) Step 4: Import contacts 35% (-15%) Step 5: Send first email 25% (-10%) ← ACTIVATION
Priority order: fix Step 1→2 first (biggest absolute drop).

rule.statement: Watch at least 20 session recordings before forming hypotheses; agents cannot replace qualitative pattern recognition from real user behavior.
rule.rationale: Funnel data shows where users drop but not why. Session recordings reveal whether the cause is confusion, missing information, performance issues, or unclear next steps.

list.item: Friction: too many form fields, slow load, required integrations → reduce, pre-fill, defer
Friction: no guidance after signup → add tooltips, coach marks, sample data
Motivation: users can activate but do not want to yet → show value earlier, add social proof
Motivation: wrong user type → segment onboarding by use case

section.name: diagnosing-low-activation
section.p: Symptom-to-cause mapping, ICE scoring for prioritization, and agent-specific gotchas.

list.item: Drop at signup step → friction: too many fields, no SSO, password requirements → reduce to email only, add magic link
Drop at onboarding → complexity: too many steps, no guidance → simplify to 3-5 steps, add templates
Drop before Aha moment → unclear next step → add tooltips, coach marks, activation checklist
Drop at core action → UX problems → user test and fix specific friction
Long time to activate → too many steps → remove non-essential steps, pre-fill defaults

rule.statement: Score every experiment hypothesis on Impact (1-10), Confidence (1-10), and Ease (1-10); prioritize by ICE average; run highest-scoring experiment first.
rule.rationale: ICE forces explicit judgment about ROI before investing engineering effort. Without scoring, teams default to easiest changes (high Ease, low Impact) rather than highest-value work.

section.reference: Python ICE scorer for ranking activation experiments

antipattern.description: Optimizing for high-completion events rather than retention-correlated behaviors.
antipattern.reason: Agents pick "completed onboarding" as activation because it has 80% completion vs "created a report" at 25%. But only the latter predicts retention. Always force the agent to score activation event candidates against D30 retention lift.

antipattern.description: Using "time-to-activation" without a fixed window definition.
antipattern.reason: Leaving the window open inflates activation rate and breaks week-over-week comparisons. Define window explicitly: e.g. "activated within 7 days of signup".

antipattern.description: Attacking the biggest funnel drop-off without confirming it is causal.
antipattern.reason: Funnel drop-offs are correlated with activation failure but not always the direct cause. Confirm with session recordings and user interviews before building the fix.

antipattern.description: Reducing signup friction before fixing downstream activation steps.
antipattern.reason: More signups with the same downstream funnel just inflates the top-of-funnel number. Fix activation rate before expanding acquisition.

section.name: agent-integration
section.p: Agentic workflow and failure modes specific to activation optimization.

On `opus`, a strategist defines/audits the activation event and reviews retention correlation. A `sonnet` analyst maps the funnel from event data, identifies top 3 drop-offs, and writes hypothesis cards (ICE-scored). A `haiku` ops agent computes weekly activation rate, segments by source/persona, and posts deltas. A `sonnet` copy agent drafts onboarding microcopy, tooltip text, and email-sequence variants for top experiments.

list.item: The agent will pick a high-completion event as "activation" because it looks healthy — force it to score against retention lift, not absolute frequency.
Funnel events with the same name across web/app but different definitions cause silent drop-offs; agent must list event-name + platform pairs.
"Time-to-activation" needs a fixed window (e.g. 7d); leaving it open inflates rate and breaks comparisons week-over-week.
Onboarding-flow tools (Appcues/Pendo) cache content; the agent must invalidate or version-tag flows when shipping copy changes.
Don't let the agent ship microcopy variants directly to production — route through a feature-flag and an A/B test.
Cross-device users get attributed twice; require the agent to use a stable user-id, not anonymous distinct_id.

template.purpose: Python ICE scorer for ranking activation experiments.
